title-group.article-title: Quaternionic Gate Fusion on S^3: Geometry-Native Optimization for Single-Qubit Circuit Segments

abstract.p: This paper studies quaternionic gate fusion as a local optimization pass for single-qubit circuit segments. Building on qqc-001-foundations and qqc-002-canonical-ir, it treats each adjacent single-qubit run as a sequence of canonical unit-quaternion representatives in SU(2) modulo global phase. Fusion is defined by quaternion multiplication in circuit order, followed by normalization and deterministic sign-canonicalization on the unit 3-sphere S^3. The resulting fused representative is unique under the fixed hemisphere convention and can therefore serve as a canonical optimized payload for the segment. The paper also defines an axis-aware reconstruction layer: exact identity outputs are removed, exact axis-aligned products recover Rx(theta), Ry(theta), or Rz(theta), known canonical tuples recover named gates such as X, Z, S, and H, and all remaining cases fall back to a generic single-qubit primitive. A small shipped benchmark harness evaluates the method on a 13-segment reference corpus covering textbook chains, mixed named-gate segments, rotation-heavy inputs, ansatz-style examples, and segments extracted from two-qubit contexts. On that measured corpus, 36 input single-qubit gates reduce to 11 reconstructed outputs while preserving exact segment action up to global phase under the canonical quaternion equivalence check. The scope is explicitly local: the paper does not optimize entangling gates, define a full compiler, or claim universal optimality.

license.license-p: This draft is distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

sec.title: 1. Introduction
sec.p: This paper studies quaternionic gate fusion as a local optimization pass for single-qubit circuit segments. The target is not a universal compiler pipeline but a deterministic simplification layer for adjacent one-qubit runs.

The key idea is that the canonical quaternionic IR introduced in paper 2 is not only descriptive but operational: adjacent single-qubit gates can be multiplied directly in quaternion form, canonicalized, and reconstructed into a simpler output.

sec.title: 2. Background and dependency on papers 1 and 2
sec.p: Paper 1 fixed the quaternion-SU(2) correspondence and clarified that single-qubit gates are modeled modulo global phase. Paper 2 defined the canonical u1q tuple, the normalization map, the sign rule q ~ -q, and the shortest-geodesic hemisphere convention.

sec.title: 3. Problem setup
sec.p: A single-qubit segment is a maximal consecutive run of single-qubit gates on one wire between barriers such as entangling gates, measurement, reset, or classical-control boundaries. The optimization problem is to replace such a segment by a simpler exact equivalent up to global phase.

sec.title: 4. Quaternionic fusion method
sec.p: Each gate in the segment is translated into a canonical unit quaternion. If the application order is g_1, ..., g_n with representatives q_1, ..., q_n, the fused payload is Can(q_n ... q_1). This multiplication order matches the fixed Phi convention inherited from paper 1.
Normalization and sign-canonicalization are then applied so that the stored fused representative is deterministic.

sec.title: 5. Axis-aware reconstruction
sec.p: Reconstruction is layered. Exact identity is removed, exact named-gate tuples are recognized first, exact single-axis quaternions recover Rx(theta), Ry(theta), or Rz(theta), and all other outputs are emitted as a generic canonical single-qubit primitive u1q.

sec.title: 6. Theoretical properties
sec.p: The method preserves the represented SU(2) action up to global phase because quaternion multiplication corresponds exactly to sequential composition. The final canonical representative is unique under the fixed hemisphere and equatorial tie-break convention.

sec.title: 7. Benchmark methodology and measured reference corpus
sec.p: The shipped benchmark harness evaluates a 13-segment reference corpus with a syntax-preserving no-fusion baseline. Metrics include original and optimized gate count, original and optimized depth, exact equivalence preservation, and reconstruction kind.

On this measured corpus, 36 input single-qubit gates reduce to 11 reconstructed outputs. All 13 segments collapse to one canonical quaternionic representative, 2 outputs simplify to identity, and 8 of 13 outputs recover as either exact named gates or exact axis-aligned rotations.

sec.title: 8. Worked examples and measured results
sec.p: Representative examples include Rx(pi/2); Rx(pi/2) reducing to X, Rz(pi/4); Rz(pi/4) reducing to S, H; Z; H reducing to X, and exact axis-aligned reconstruction cases such as Rz(pi/5); Rz(pi/10) reducing to Rz(0.3 pi).

sec.title: 9. Standard mathematics vs RQM Technologies framing
sec.p: The standard ingredients are U(2), SU(2), unit quaternions, the sign ambiguity q ~ -q, and quaternion multiplication. The project-specific contribution is the local optimization framing: canonical single-qubit segment extraction, deterministic fusion, and layered reconstruction for compiler use.

sec.title: 10. Scope, limitations, and non-claims
sec.p: This paper does not optimize entangling gates, define a full compiler, prove universal superiority over external baselines, claim hardware speedups, or solve the general synthesis problem for arbitrary gate sets. It defines only a local optimization pass for single-qubit segments.

sec.title: 11. Conclusion
sec.p: Quaternionic representation is operationally useful because it turns adjacent single-qubit fusion into direct quaternion multiplication followed by deterministic canonicalization. The measured reference corpus shows that this local pass can shrink single-qubit segments substantially while preserving exact segment action up to global phase.

ref.mixed-citation: Cross, Andrew W.; Javadi-Abhari, Ali; Alexander, Thomas; de Beaudrap, Niel; Bishop, Lev S.; Heidel, Steven; Ryan, Colm A.; Sivarajah, Prasahnt; Smolin, John A.; Gambetta, Jay M.; Johnson, Blake R. OpenQASM 3: A Broader and Deeper Quantum Assembly Language. ACM Transactions on Quantum Computing. 2022; 3(3):12. doi:10.1145/3505636
Hall, Brian C. Lie Groups, Lie Algebras, and Representations: An Elementary Introduction. 2nd ed. Springer; 2015.
Kuipers, Jack B. Quaternions and Rotation Sequences: A Primer with Applications to Orbits, Aerospace, and Virtual Reality. Princeton University Press; 1999.
Nielsen, Michael A.; Chuang, Isaac L. Quantum Computation and Quantum Information. 10th Anniversary Edition. Cambridge University Press; 2010.
Smith, Robert S.; Curtis, Michael J.; Zeng, William J. A Practical Quantum Instruction Set Architecture. arXiv preprint arXiv:1608.03355. 2016.
